input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator**, designed to regulate and maintain a target temperature within an insulated container. The system comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize heat loss.
  - **Heating Element**: A heatbed responsible for generating and distributing thermal energy inside the container.
  - **Air Circulation**: A fan to ensure uniform temperature distribution within the box.
  - **Sensors**:
    - **Two internal temperature sensors** for real-time monitoring of the internal environment.
    - **One external temperature sensor** for ambient condition tracking.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the central controller for processing sensor data and executing regulatory actions.
  The operational domain involves **temperature regulation** (e.g., maintaining a setpoint temperature regardless of internal content or external disturbances). The system is suitable for digital twinning due to its **dynamic thermal behavior**, **sensor-driven feedback loops**, and **requirement for predictive and adaptive control** to ensure safety, efficiency, and robustness in varying environmental conditions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous acquisition of sensor data (internal/external temperatures) from the **Physical Twin (PT)** via embedded sensors, enabling **state estimation** and **anomaly detection**.
  - **Dynamic Simulation**: Execution of **physics-based models** (e.g., differential equations) to replicate and predict the **thermal behavior** of the PT under current or hypothetical operating conditions.
  - **Calibration & Model Refinement**: Use of **calibration algorithms** to update **cyber-physical models** (e.g., thermal dynamics) with real-time sensor data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT.
  - **Safety & Performance Validation**: **Monitoring algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers) to assess whether the PT operates within safe and optimal bounds (e.g., temperature thresholds).
  - **Predictive Analytics**: Generation of **predictive insights** (e.g., future temperature trajectories) to preemptively address deviations or failures.
  - **Optimization & Control**: Integration with **optimization algorithms** (e.g., state estimators, controllers) to dynamically adjust the PT’s actuators (e.g., heatbed power, fan speed) based on DT-derived recommendations.
  - **Visualization**: **Advanced visualizations** of system states, model predictions, and operational metrics for engineers or operators.
  **Target Users**:
  - **Engineers** (for design validation, fault diagnosis, and optimization).
  - **Operators** (for real-time decision-making and maintenance planning).
  - **Automated Controllers** (for closed-loop adjustments via supervisory algorithms).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the **Digital Twin (DT)** for the incubator leverages the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, as inferred from the case study and general DT frameworks:
  - **Hardware Platform**:
    - **Raspberry Pi** (embedded controller for real-time data acquisition and actuation).
    - **Temperature Sensors** (e.g., DS18B20 or equivalent) for internal/external measurements.
  - **Software & Development Tools**:
    - **Python-based frameworks** (e.g., for model calibration, simulation, and algorithm execution).
    - **Open-source repositories** (e.g., [INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) for hardware/software specifications and experimental validation.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Physics-based models** (e.g., differential equations for thermal dynamics) implemented via numerical solvers (e.g., SciPy, MATLAB/Simulink).
    - **Calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters using real-time sensor data.
  - **Data Management**:
    - **Time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for storing sensor data and model outputs.
    - **Data pipelines** to process raw sensor inputs into actionable insights.
  - **Control & Optimization Algorithms**:
    - **Supervisory controllers** (e.g., PID, MPC) for closed-loop temperature regulation.
    - **Fault detection modules** to identify deviations from expected behavior.
  - **Visualization Tools**:
    - **Dashboards** (e.g., Grafana, MATLAB App Designer) for real-time monitoring of system states and predictive analytics.
  - **Interoperability Enablers**:
    - **Standardized communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) for seamless data exchange between sensors, controllers, and DT components.
    - **Ontology-based modelling** (implicitly referenced in Document 1) to structure knowledge about the PT’s cyber-physical aspects and environment.
  The **Incubator case study** specifically highlights the use of **two calibrated models** (Section 3) and **experimental validation** (Section 4) to demonstrate DT capabilities, though exact simulation engines or databases are not explicitly named in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin (DT) system operates with a **real-time synchronization paradigm**, where data from the Physical Twin (PT) is continuously collected via sensors (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator case study) and fed into the DT framework. The temporal granularity depends on the **sampling rate of sensors** and the **latency requirements of calibration algorithms**, which update the DT models (e.g., differential equations for dynamic behavior) to reflect the PT's current state. For instance, in the incubator example, temperature sensors provide **continuous monitoring**, enabling the DT to adjust its predictive and control models (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) with minimal delay. The system must account for **latency constraints** to ensure timely detection of deviations (e.g., unsafe or suboptimal performance) and trigger corrective actions (e.g., via supervisory controllers or human intervention). The time scale for model updates and simulation runs is influenced by the **trade-off between accuracy and computational speed**, as high-fidelity models (e.g., detailed differential equations) may require longer simulation times, while real-time operation demands optimized, lower-fidelity approximations for critical services like safety monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports **multiple instances and hierarchical structures** to manage complexity and scalability. In the incubator case study, a **single DT instance** is built for the Physical Twin (PT), but the architecture can be extended to **multiple DTs** for different subsystems or environments (e.g., decentralized DTs for individual components like the heatbed or fan). The system employs a **centralized coordination mechanism** where calibration algorithms aggregate sensor data from the PT (e.g., temperature sensors) and update shared models (e.g., cyber-physical system (CPS) dynamics). However, the documents do not specify **decentralized architectures** or explicit coordination protocols (e.g., distributed consensus algorithms) between multiple DT instances. The scope of each DT instance is defined by its **responsibility for specific models and algorithms** (e.g., one instance may focus on thermal dynamics, another on fault detection), with **interdependencies managed via data exchange** (e.g., passing updated parameters or state estimates between modules).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **modular and service-oriented orchestration pattern**, integrating **three core components**: (1) **Data collection** (via sensors from the PT), (2) **Models** (representing cyber and physical aspects of the PT and its environment, e.g., differential equations for dynamics or empirical models for calibration), and (3) **Algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, and optimizers). The architecture employs a **closed-loop control structure** (as depicted in Figure 1), where calibration algorithms continuously refine models using real-time sensor data. This updated data is then fed into **monitoring services** to evaluate system performance (e.g., safety and optimality). When violations are detected, the system triggers **corrective actions** (e.g., adjustments to the PT via actuators or human intervention). The orchestration relies on **tight coupling between cyber and physical components**, with resource management focused on balancing **model fidelity** (e.g., accuracy of differential equations) and **computational efficiency** (e.g., simulation speed for real-time decision-making). The system-wide coordination is achieved through **shared data buses** and **algorithm pipelines**, ensuring seamless interaction between modeling, simulation, and control services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment. **Model types** include:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: Abstract representations of physical components (e.g., CAD models of a car’s chassis, wheels, or an incubator’s insulated box and heatbed), capturing static attributes like dimensions and shapes.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Described by differential equations (e.g., thermal models predicting temperature dynamics in the incubator case study) to simulate physical interactions (e.g., forces on a car or heat transfer in an incubator). The incubator case study employed **two simulation models**—one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**—to predict internal temperature, with the latter demonstrating superior accuracy in modeling thermal inertia.
  - **Analytical Models**: Mathematical formulations (e.g., state estimators, optimizers) derived from first principles or empirical data to infer system behavior under varying conditions.

  **Data sources** originate from the **Physical Twin (PT)**, collected via **sensors** (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator) over time, and include:
  - **Real-time operational data** (e.g., environmental conditions, actuator states, sensor readings).
  - **Historical data** for model calibration and validation.

  **Data management approaches** involve:
  - **Calibration algorithms** to update models (e.g., adjusting parameters in the incubator’s thermal model using sensor data to minimize prediction errors).
  - **Integration with monitoring systems** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers) to ensure the DT reflects the PT’s current state.
  - **Model relationships**: Hierarchical or modular structures where models interact (e.g., a dynamic model of a car’s suspension may depend on a geometric model of its chassis).

  **Roles in the DT constellation**:
  - **Simulation**: Reproduce and predict PT behavior under current/anticipated conditions (e.g., thermal simulations in the incubator).
  - **Optimization**: Algorithms (e.g., optimizers) use data and models to propose improvements (e.g., adjusting control parameters for energy efficiency).
  - **Decision support**: Monitors (e.g., safety/performance checkers) trigger corrective actions when deviations exceed thresholds (e.g., recalibrating models or alerting operators).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in a Digital Twin system include **actuators** and **control mechanisms** that interact with the Physical Twin (PT) to influence its behavior. Key aspects include:
  - **Types of actuators**: Not explicitly detailed in the documents, but inferred from the **incubator case study** (a CPS example) to include **electromechanical actuators** (e.g., a **heatbed** for temperature control) and potentially **electronic control units (ECUs)** in automotive systems (e.g., throttle actuators, braking systems).
  - **Control capabilities**: Implemented via **embedded computers** and **networked controllers** (e.g., the incubator’s **software system for communication and control**), which execute algorithms to regulate PT behavior (e.g., maintaining a set temperature in the incubator).
  - **Remote control interfaces**: Enabled through **cyber-physical communication protocols** (e.g., Controller Area Network (CAN) in cars or proprietary protocols in the incubator) to transmit commands from the DT to actuators.
  - **Actuation ranges and limitations**:
    - **Incubator example**: The heatbed’s output is constrained by physical laws (e.g., maximum power dissipation) and environmental factors (e.g., ambient temperature).
    - **Automotive example**: Actuators (e.g., steering, braking) have defined **operational ranges** (e.g., torque limits, angular velocities) and **safety constraints** (e.g., anti-lock braking systems).
  - **Safety constraints**: Hardcoded or algorithmically enforced limits (e.g., **fault detectors** in the DT that halt actuation if anomalies are detected, such as overheating in the incubator or sensor failures in a car).
  - **Addressing mechanisms**: Actuators are identified via **unique identifiers** (e.g., CAN IDs in automotive systems) or **logical addresses** in the incubator’s control software to ensure precise targeting of commands.
  - **Not in Document**: Specific technologies (e.g., servo motors, pneumatic actuators) or detailed actuation protocols (e.g., Modbus, OPC UA) are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **sensing infrastructure** in a Digital Twin system comprises **sensors** and **data acquisition systems** that monitor the Physical Twin (PT) and its environment. Key technical details from the documents include:
  - **Sensor types**:
    - **Environmental sensors**: Measure external conditions (e.g., **temperature sensors** in the incubator’s case study to track ambient and internal temperatures).
    - **Process sensors**: Monitor PT-specific variables (e.g., **strain gauges** for force measurement in a car’s suspension, inferred from dynamic modeling requirements).
    - **State sensors**: Provide real-time feedback on PT components (e.g., **position sensors** for wheels, **current sensors** for the incubator’s heatbed).
  - **Measurement capabilities**:
    - **Incubator case study**: Focuses on **thermal measurements** (e.g., predicting internal temperature with models calibrated using sensor data).
    - **Automotive example**: Sensors may include **inertial measurement units (IMUs)**, **pressure sensors**, and **accelerometers** to capture dynamic behavior.
  - **Spatial distribution**: Sensors are strategically placed to cover critical PT aspects (e.g., **multiple temperature sensors** in the incubator’s insulated box to ensure uniform monitoring; **distributed sensors** in a car’s chassis for structural health monitoring).
  - **Sampling frequencies**: Not explicitly quantified in the documents, but implied to be **high enough for real-time monitoring** (e.g., thermal models require frequent updates to reflect transient conditions like heatbed activation/deactivation).
  - **Data transmission rates**: Dependent on the **communication infrastructure** (e.g., **CAN bus** in cars or **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth** in the incubator), with rates tailored to sensor output (e.g., **millisecond-level updates** for critical actuators like brakes).
  - **Accuracy and precision specifications**:
    - **Incubator example**: The **four-parameter thermal model** achieved higher fidelity by better capturing **thermal inertia**, suggesting sensors must have **high precision** (e.g., ±0.1°C resolution for temperature sensors, though exact values are not provided).
    - **Automotive example**: Sensors like **IMUs** may require **sub-millimeter precision** for dynamic modeling.
  - **Data collection mechanisms**:
    - **Continuous streaming**: Real-time data is fed into the DT for immediate processing (e.g., fault detection, model calibration).
    - **Batch processing**: Historical data is stored for **offline analysis** (e.g., validating model predictions against sensor logs).
    - **Protocol compliance**: Data is transmitted via standardized or proprietary protocols (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT**) to ensure interoperability with the DT’s algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of a Digital Twin system are ensured through rigorous **model validation**, **uncertainty quantification**, and **quality assurance processes**. Key technical aspects from the documents include:
  - **Model fidelity requirements**:
    - **Complexity vs. accuracy trade-off**: The incubator case study demonstrated that a **four-parameter thermal model** outperformed a **two-parameter model** in predicting **thermal inertia**, highlighting the need for models to balance complexity with representational accuracy.
    - **Domain-specific fidelity**: Models must align with the PT’s **physical laws** (e.g., differential equations for dynamic systems) and **operational constraints** (e.g., sensor limitations).
  - **Validation and verification methods**:
    - **Empirical validation**: Models are tested against **real-world sensor data** (e.g., comparing predicted vs. measured temperatures in the incubator).
    - **Simulation benchmarking**: Models are validated by replicating **known physical phenomena** (e.g., transient heating/cooling cycles in the incubator).
    - **Cross-model comparison**: Alternative models (e.g., two-parameter vs. four-parameter) are evaluated to select the most accurate representation.
  - **Uncertainty sources and quantification**:
    - **Sensor noise**: Measurement inaccuracies (e.g., ±0.1°C drift in temperature sensors) propagate into model predictions.
    - **Parameter estimation errors**: Free parameters in models (e.g., thermal conductivity in the incubator) are subject to **calibration uncertainty**.
    - **Environmental variability**: Unmodeled factors (e.g., wind in automotive systems, ambient temperature fluctuations in the incubator) introduce uncertainty.
    - **Quantification techniques**: Statistical methods (e.g., **Monte Carlo simulations**, **Bayesian inference**) are implied to estimate uncertainty bounds for model outputs.
  - **Error detection and correction mechanisms**:
    - **Real-time monitoring**: Algorithms (e.g., **fault detectors**) compare PT sensor data against model predictions to identify discrepancies (e.g., sudden temperature spikes in the incubator).
    - **Automatic recalibration**: Calibration algorithms adjust model parameters (e.g., thermal inertia coefficients) using **online learning** techniques to minimize prediction errors.
    - **Human-in-the-loop**: Operators intervene when automated corrections fail (e.g., manual override in the incubator’s control system).
  - **Quality assurance processes**:
    - **Continuous verification**: Models are periodically revalidated against updated sensor data to ensure long-term accuracy.
    - **Traceability**: Documentation links models to their **validation datasets** and **assumptions** (e.g., the incubator’s model parameters are tied to specific sensor calibration curves).
    - **Compliance with standards**: While not explicitly stated, the DT’s processes likely adhere to **industry standards** (e.g., ISO 15926 for ontology modeling, IEC 61131 for PLC-based systems in the incubator).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator involves real-time data acquisition from the **Physical Twin (PT)** via **sensors** (specifically, **two temperature sensors inside the styrofoam box** and **one outside**) to monitor environmental conditions. The data types transmitted include **temperature readings** (likely in °C or °F) with **time-series granularity**, collected continuously or at predefined intervals (frequency not explicitly stated but implied to be high-resolution for dynamic control). The communication protocol is not explicitly detailed, but the system integrates with a **Raspberry Pi** (acting as the cyber-physical system controller), suggesting a **local wired/wireless (e.g., Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or serial communication) connection** to transmit sensor data to the DT. **Preprocessing steps** may include calibration (as referenced in the DT framework) to adjust for sensor inaccuracies or environmental noise, ensuring data quality assurance via **calibration algorithms** that update the DT’s models (e.g., differential equations or empirical models) with the best parameter estimates. Data integration mechanisms involve **fusion of sensor inputs** (e.g., averaging or weighted aggregation of internal/external temperature readings) to inform the DT’s monitors for safety and optimality checks. Event triggers for data transmission are likely **time-based (periodic polling)** or **threshold-based (e.g., temperature deviations exceeding predefined limits)**.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT system involves **control commands** generated by the DT’s algorithms (e.g., supervisory controllers, optimizers) and transmitted to the **Physical Twin (PT)** via the **Raspberry Pi controller**. Command types include **actuation signals** to adjust the **heatbed power** and **fan speed** to regulate internal temperature. The control message format is not explicitly specified but likely follows a **structured protocol** (e.g., JSON, CSV, or binary) compatible with the Pi’s input/output interfaces (e.g., GPIO or PWM for analog control). **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not detailed, but the DT framework implies **safety checks** (e.g., verifying commands against operational constraints) before execution. Feedback systems include **closed-loop monitoring** via the temperature sensors, where the DT’s monitors detect deviations (e.g., unsafe or suboptimal performance) and trigger corrective actions. **Conflict resolution** may involve prioritizing commands based on predefined rules (e.g., safety overrides optimization) or human intervention, as suggested by the DT’s adaptive decision-making process. Command execution confirmation is implied via **sensor feedback loops** (e.g., the DT observes the PT’s response to commands and updates its models accordingly).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for the incubator DT is centered around a **local-area network (LAN) topology**, with the **Raspberry Pi** acting as the primary cyber-physical interface between the PT and DT. Communication protocols are not explicitly stated but likely include **standard IoT protocols** (e.g., **MQTT for lightweight messaging**, **HTTP/REST for configuration**, or **serial/UART for direct sensor-Pi communication**). The network architecture is **star-topology**, with sensors (temperature probes) connected to the Pi, which then interfaces with the DT’s computational backend (potentially cloud/edge-hosted). **Bandwidth requirements** are low (transmitting temperature readings at high frequency but with minimal payload size), but **latency must be minimized** to ensure real-time control (e.g., <100ms for closed-loop stability). **Reliability mechanisms** include **redundant sensor readings** (two internal temperature sensors) and **calibration algorithms** to mitigate single-point failures. **Security protocols** are not detailed but may involve **encryption (TLS/SSL for cloud communication)** and **authentication (API keys or certificates)** if the DT interacts with external systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the incubator DT is not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the system implies a **hybrid deployment model** combining **edge computing (Raspberry Pi)** and **potential cloud/on-premise components**. The Pi handles **real-time data acquisition, preprocessing, and local control logic**, while higher-level DT functions (e.g., advanced simulations, long-term optimization) may reside in a **cloud or on-premise server**. **Computational requirements** are modest for the Pi (e.g., running calibration algorithms and basic control loops), but the DT’s backend may require **scalable resources** (e.g., virtual machines or containers) to handle complex models or large-scale simulations. **Storage requirements** include **time-series sensor data** (likely stored in a lightweight database like SQLite or InfluxDB on the Pi) and **model parameters** (e.g., differential equation coefficients) updated via calibration. **Scalability mechanisms** are not specified but could involve **containerization (Docker)** or **microservices** for modular DT components. **Availability/reliability assurance** relies on the Pi’s local redundancy (e.g., sensor fusion) and potentially **cloud failover** for critical DT functions. The deployment model supports **on-premise edge deployment** (Pi + local sensors) with optional **cloud integration** for advanced analytics.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle stages, as inferred from the provided documents, include **pre-deployment modeling and simulation phases**, where abstract representations (e.g., CAD models for physical geometry or differential equation-based dynamic models for behavior analysis) are developed to understand and optimize system aspects such as the incubator’s temperature regulation. During **deployment**, the DT integrates **real-time data collection** from the Physical Twin (PT) via sensors (e.g., two internal and one external temperature sensors in the incubator case study) and **calibration algorithms** to refine models (e.g., updating parameters of Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and environmental models) for accuracy. Post-deployment, the DT operates in a **monitoring and control phase**, where calibrated models inform **safety and performance monitors** (e.g., detecting violations in temperature setpoints or operational constraints). The lifecycle also includes **evolutionary phases**, where the DT adapts to changes in the PT or environment (e.g., reconfigurability for robustness) via iterative updates to models and algorithms, potentially involving human or automated interventions (e.g., adjusting control parameters or triggering maintenance actions). Governance is implied through the structured integration of **data, models, and algorithms** (as per Fig. 1), ensuring traceability and consistency across phases, though explicit governance frameworks (e.g., ISO standards or compliance protocols) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the provided context follows a **model-driven, iterative engineering methodology** rooted in **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) principles**. Key stages include:
  1. **Requirements Analysis**: Defining goals (e.g., achieving and maintaining a target temperature in the incubator) and identifying critical components (e.g., heatbed, fan, sensors).
  2. **Model Development**: Creating **multi-abstraction models** (e.g., geometric CAD models for physical structure and differential equation-based models for dynamic behavior) to represent the PT and its environment. The incubator case study explicitly mentions **two models with calibration methods**, suggesting a focus on parameter tuning for accuracy.
  3. **Data Integration**: Collecting **real-time sensor data** (e.g., temperature readings from internal/external sensors) to populate the DT’s data layer.
  4. **Algorithm Implementation**: Deploying **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation techniques) to align models with observed PT behavior, followed by **monitoring algorithms** (e.g., safety checks for temperature deviations) and **control algorithms** (e.g., supervisory controllers for corrective actions).
  5. **Validation and Testing**: Conducting **experimental validation** (as referenced in Section 4 of Document 2) to verify DT predictions against PT performance, including edge cases (e.g., environmental changes or sensor failures).
  Evolution management is achieved through **continuous calibration and reconfiguration**, where the DT adapts to PT changes (e.g., varying thermal loads) via iterative model updates. Version control is implied but not explicitly detailed; however, the **publicly available repository** ([GitHub link in Document 2](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) suggests versioned hardware/software specifications are maintained for reproducibility. Quality assurance practices are not explicitly described beyond the calibration and validation steps.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin (DT) provides **real-time and predictive insights** through a **data-driven, model-informed decision-making pipeline**. Key capabilities include:
  - **Behavioral Analysis**: Simulations reproduce and predict PT behavior (e.g., temperature dynamics in the incubator) under current or hypothetical conditions, leveraging calibrated models (e.g., differential equations for thermal systems).
  - **Anomaly Detection**: Monitors (as per Fig. 1) analyze real-time data to identify **safety violations** (e.g., temperature exceeding thresholds) or **performance deviations** (e.g., inefficient heating due to fan failure).
  - **Optimization Recommendations**: Algorithms (e.g., state estimators, optimizers) generate actionable insights, such as **adjusting control parameters** (e.g., heatbed power) or **triggering maintenance** (e.g., recalibrating sensors).
  - **User Interaction**: Decisions are communicated to human operators or automated systems (e.g., via alerts or control signals), with **confidence measures** implied through model calibration accuracy (e.g., parameter uncertainty quantification). The incubator case study’s experimental results (Section 4) suggest empirical validation of these insights, though explicit confidence metrics (e.g., prediction intervals) are not provided. Visualization tools (e.g., advanced visualizations mentioned in Document 2) are inferred but not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its integration of **monitoring algorithms** that verify the system's performance against predefined safety thresholds. Specifically, the DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment, ensuring accurate parameter estimation. These updated models are then fed into **monitors** that assess whether the system operates within safe and optimal boundaries. In cases of detected violations (e.g., temperature deviations in the incubator case study), the system triggers **human or algorithmic interventions** to mitigate risks. The DT framework also implicitly supports **fail-safe mechanisms** by enabling predictive simulations to preemptively identify potential failures or unsafe conditions. However, explicit details on **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication systems), **access control mechanisms**, or **compliance with data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) are not provided in the documents. The system’s reliance on **sensor-collected data** (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator) and **model-driven decision-making** suggests a need for robust data integrity and confidentiality, though no specific technologies (e.g., blockchain for audit trails, role-based access control) are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
